answer_raw: DECOMPOSE: In FY2025, Productivity and Business Processes generated $120.810 billion of revenue and $69.773 billion of operating income, with Microsoft 365 Commercial products and cloud services at $87.767 billion, LinkedIn at $17.812 billion, Dynamics products and cloud services at $7.827 billion, and Microsoft 365 Consumer products and cloud services at $7.404 billion (FY2025 10-K, Note 18 – Segment Information and Geographic Data). Intelligent Cloud generated $106.265 billion of revenue and $44.589 billion of operating income, dominated by Server products and cloud services at $98.435 billion versus Enterprise and partner services at $7.760 billion (FY2025 10-K, Note 18). More Personal Computing generated $54.649 billion of revenue and $14.166 billion of operating income, including Gaming at $23.455 billion, Windows and Devices at $17.314 billion, and Search and news advertising at $13.878 billion (FY2025 10-K, Note 18). APPLY: For Azure and AI infrastructure, AI is creating new demand: Server products and cloud services revenue increased 23% in FY2025, driven by Azure and other cloud services revenue growth of 34% (FY2025 10-K, Part II, Item 7, MD&A, Intelligent Cloud), and Q2 FY2026 Azure and other cloud services revenue grew 39% in constant currency while demand continued to exceed supply (Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026 prepared remarks). Complementarity is strong because Microsoft describes agents as the new apps requiring model catalogs, tuning, orchestration, context engineering, safety, observability, management, and security, while Fabric exceeded a $2 billion annualized revenue run rate, had over 31,000 customers, and revenue was up 60% year over year (Satya Nadella, Q2 FY2026 prepared remarks). Substitution risk is real because Microsoft discloses that Azure faces competition from cloud service providers and open-source offerings, and AI offerings compete with hyperscalers, emerging competitors, and open-source offerings (FY2025 10-K, Part I, Item 1, Intelligent Cloud – Competition). Supply constraints are both moat and risk: Microsoft says datacenters depend on permitted and buildable land, predictable energy, networking supplies, and servers including GPUs (FY2025 10-K, Part I, Item 1, Operations), and Q2 FY2026 capital expenditures were $37.5 billion, roughly two-thirds for short-lived assets such as GPUs and CPUs (Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026 prepared remarks). For Microsoft 365, AI appears to expand ARPU rather than replace the core franchise: FY2025 Microsoft 365 Commercial cloud revenue grew 15% and seats grew 6% (FY2025 10-K, Part II, Item 7, MD&A, Productivity and Business Processes), while Q2 FY2026 Microsoft 365 Commercial cloud revenue grew 17% in constant currency and paid seats grew 6% to over 450 million (Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026 prepared remarks). Copilot provides direct monetization evidence: Microsoft 365 Copilot reached 15 million paid seats, seat additions were up over 160% year over year, and the number of customers with over 35,000 seats tripled year over year (Satya Nadella, Q2 FY2026 prepared remarks). The offsetting substitution risk is that Office competes with software and global application vendors, web-based and mobile application companies, AI-first application companies, and local developers (FY2025 10-K, Part I, Item 1, Productivity and Business Processes – Competition). LinkedIn and Dynamics are more mixed but constructive: FY2025 LinkedIn revenue increased 9%, Dynamics products and cloud services revenue increased 15%, and Dynamics 365 revenue grew 19% (FY2025 10-K, Part II, Item 7, MD&A, Productivity and Business Processes); in Q2 FY2026 LinkedIn revenue increased 11% in constant currency and Dynamics 365 revenue increased 19% in constant currency (Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026 prepared remarks). For Search and news advertising, AI can improve engagement through Copilot, Edge, and Bing, and FY2025 search and news advertising revenue excluding TAC increased 20% (FY2025 10-K, Part II, Item 7, MD&A, More Personal Computing), but Microsoft also discloses search competition from search engines, websites, social platforms, and portals (FY2025 10-K, Part I, Item 1, More Personal Computing – Competition), and Q2 FY2026 search and news advertising ex-TAC growth moderated to 10% in constant currency with execution challenges (Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026 prepared remarks). Windows and Devices are strategically useful but less pure AI upside: FY2025 Windows OEM and Devices revenue increased 3%, driven by Windows OEM and offset partly by Devices (FY2025 10-K, Part II, Item 7, MD&A, More Personal Computing), while Windows 11 reached 1 billion users, up over 45% year over year (Satya Nadella, Q2 FY2026 prepared remarks). Gaming is the weakest AI-diffusion unit in the disclosed evidence: FY2025 Gaming revenue increased 9% and Xbox content and services revenue increased 16% (FY2025 10-K, Part II, Item 7, MD&A, More Personal Computing), but in Q2 FY2026 Gaming revenue decreased 9% in constant currency and Xbox content and services revenue decreased 5% in constant currency (Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026 prepared remarks). SYNTHESIZE: Assuming AI value accrues primarily to integrated platforms that control enterprise data, developer workflows, identity/security, and scarce compute, Microsoft's long-term revenue trajectory is positive because Azure consumption, Microsoft 365 Copilot ARPU, GitHub Copilot, Dynamics agents, and security workloads reinforce each other. Assuming capacity investments are monetized over useful lives, capacity can be a moat; Amy Hood said many GPU contracts are sold for the entire useful life and margins improve with time (Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026 Q&A with Mark Moerdler). The margin outlook is mixed: FY2025 Microsoft Cloud gross margin percentage decreased to 69% due to scaling AI infrastructure, partly offset by Azure efficiencies (FY2025 10-K, Part II, Item 7, Summary Results of Operations), Q2 FY2026 company gross margin percentage was 68% and Microsoft Cloud gross margin percentage was 67% (Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026 prepared remarks), and Q3 FY2026 Microsoft Cloud gross margin percentage was guided to roughly 65% due to continued AI investment (Amy Hood, Q2 FY2026 outlook). My thesis is therefore bullish on long-term revenue and platform durability, but only moderately bullish on margins and ROIC until AI infrastructure utilization, pricing, and contract duration consistently offset the higher capital intensity.
